video_title: This Is What an AI Unwind Looks Like
transcript: All right, welcome to the risk reversal podcast. I am Dan Nathan. That is Guy Adami. Hi, Guy. >> Hello. Big week coming up, man. We just actually got done with a big week of earnings. Bank stocks got it kicked off. A lot of stuff going on in the memory and the uh semis. Uh we have some model wars, not just here, but also abroad. Some good stuff coming on um from China that we got to talk about. Apple in China, that's one. It was not on my bingo card for this week. oil guy. Oil up 22% since hitting a low. It was like a multi-month low, right? Since the the war started and some of those stocks are still trading pretty well. >> Netflix, this is Friday. We are talking about 11 o'clock a.m. Stocks down 9% fall. It's its earnings. And then we'll just do uh a little earnings preview, that sort of thing. How's all that sound? >> Sounds good. And you we might as well start with memory because those are the ones that have moved uh in an amazing fashion. And this is something we've been addressing now I think for the better part of the last three or four weeks where not every day but I'd say three times a week we are seeing 8% moves to the upside and to the downside and obviously everybody's love child over the last six months maybe longer it's been Micron and you know a stock that recently made an all-time high of $1,255 and I think it did it June 25th or something like that. So, as we're sitting here, um, the third week of July or what have you, a stock that today, and again, today is Friday, traded down to $800. So, so think about that move for a second. It's a pretty significant move in a very short period of time. Now, we've been saying that if you're looking for support, it probably comes in the form of the former all-time high, believe it or not, that we saw back in May, which lo and behold, Dan, was $800. So today's low mirrors the alltime the prior all-time high that we saw in May. So that sort of makes sense. But the bigger picture here is is this just a sell off like we've seen before over the last you know 6 to9 months or is this something more? By the time you're listening to this you will have a sense. I mean it was kind of ugly on the opening. We looked at this DRAM ETF which I absolutely love. We've been talking about it. um a lot recently. This was listed I think you and I talked about a few weeks ago in April is very near the all-time highs in many of these um stocks or at least how the sentiment was turning a little bit. This ETF guy has 22 billion dollars under management at highs. I think it was over 25. I I can't remember the last time I have seen a sector ETF grow like this. But when I'm looking at the price action in a bunch of these names and this this SKH highix is going to be really confusing by the way just the way it trades in in in Korea and then how it trades here obviously the ADR there's a lot of dislocations here but all those names that we just talked about Samsung Micron SK they're all in this DRM ETF this thing today traded as low as 4865 is trading at 53 right now okay so a big big reversal and at that high going back to let's say early mid June it was trading at 80. Um, okay. So, you know, we know what's going on here. We know what Seagate, Western Digital, Micron, Samsung, and SK make up 40 45% of this thing, but it really looks like it's trying to make a spike bottom. So, it'll be interesting to see how this stuff closes. The SMH and the socks still down 2%, guy. And one of the things that I found really interesting is that we had this rotation this week out of memory and semis and into hyperscalers. Now, that has reversed a little bit because Thursday afternoon, Google, it was a report from Bloomberg that was saying that they're pushing out their latest version of Gemini, which is their coding model. Okay, there's other models that have been out with Gemini of late, and the stock sold off 5% in a straight line. Okay, as we're recording right now, the stock is off two and a half percent. It was uh down uh little earlier on the opening or a little further on the opening. It was about a 10% two-day sell-off. Okay, that's pretty astounding. And so that reverses this little rotation narrative. And so how did you buy it? I know we were talking about it on the pods. We were talking about it on fast money. You know, we've seen this kind of back and forth between semis memory and then software, right? But now it seems to be the hyperscalers joined the party. By the way, you know, Google moves both to the upside and the downside. It's amazing that a $4 trillion dollar stock uh market cap has the moves that we've seen. But again, a stock that was $274, I think, in lows of March, it made an all-time high, I want to say, in May 408, then traded all the way down to I think three and a quarter or so. Is their search business still in the same position it's been over the last, you know, 6 to9 months? A position of strength, by the way. And then you sort of throw YouTube in the mix. And the short answer is, I think yes. I I think you can still make a pretty decent argument for Google on valuation. And I think given the selloff we've seen, as I said, since the highs, the all-time highs in early May, I think the setup is pretty good. Gross margins are expected to go from this year's 71% to about 65%. Think about that. To 65%. Now, what's weighing on that? Capex is a big part of that, right? And so, we go back to the whole notion of like, is this capex spend, which is hundreds of billions of dollars cumulatively right now for Google, using a lot of their free cash flow or most of it to do that? That's the thing that sounds so durable, right, by a lot of these companies that are booking these deals. That's the opposite of that, right?
